title: weil
summary: The most common way to say 'because' in German. Changes word order — the verb moves to the end.

examples.de: Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Berlin arbeiten möchte.
examples.en: I'm learning German because I want to work in Berlin.

examples.de: Sie bleibt zu Hause, weil sie krank ist.
examples.en: She stays at home because she is sick.

examples.de: Wir nehmen ein Taxi, weil es regnet.
examples.en: We're taking a taxi because it's raining.

examples.de: Er hat den Job bekommen, weil er sehr gut Deutsch spricht.
examples.en: He got the job because he speaks German very well.

sentenceStructure.notes: The conjugated verb always goes to the END of the weil-clause. This is the most important rule. In spoken German, some speakers use normal word order after 'weil', but this is considered informal.

usageTips: Always place the conjugated verb at the end of the weil-clause
In spoken German, you may hear 'weil' with normal word order — this is informal and not standard
Can start a sentence: 'Weil es regnet, nehmen wir ein Taxi' (note the inversion in the main clause)

commonMistakes.incorrect: Ich bleibe zu Hause, weil ich bin krank.
commonMistakes.correct: Ich bleibe zu Hause, weil ich krank bin.
commonMistakes.explanation: After 'weil', the conjugated verb must go to the very end of the clause.

title: denn
summary: An alternative way to say 'because' that keeps normal word order. Slightly more formal than 'weil'.

examples.de: Ich bleibe zu Hause, denn ich bin krank.
examples.en: I'm staying home, for I am sick.

examples.de: Wir müssen uns beeilen, denn der Zug fährt bald ab.
examples.en: We have to hurry, for the train is leaving soon.

examples.de: Er liest viel, denn er möchte Schriftsteller werden.
examples.en: He reads a lot, for he wants to become a writer.

examples.de: Sie nimmt einen Regenschirm mit, denn es soll regnen.
examples.en: She's taking an umbrella along, for it's supposed to rain.

sentenceStructure.notes: Unlike 'weil', 'denn' does NOT change the word order. The verb stays in its normal position (position 2). 'Denn' cannot start a sentence — it always connects two main clauses.

usageTips: 'Denn' cannot start a sentence — it must connect two clauses
Word order stays normal after 'denn' (subject + verb)
More common in written German and formal speech
Cannot be used as a one-word answer to 'Warum?' — use 'weil' instead

commonMistakes.incorrect: Ich lerne Deutsch, denn ich nach Berlin ziehe.
commonMistakes.correct: Ich lerne Deutsch, denn ich ziehe nach Berlin.
commonMistakes.explanation: 'denn' is a coordinating conjunction, so the word order stays completely normal (Subject + Verb).

title: deshalb
summary: Means 'therefore' or 'that's why'. Introduces the result or consequence. Causes verb-second inversion.

examples.de: Es regnet, deshalb bleibe ich zu Hause.
examples.en: It's raining, therefore I'm staying home.

examples.de: Ich habe morgen eine Prüfung, deshalb muss ich heute lernen.
examples.en: I have an exam tomorrow, that's why I have to study today.

examples.de: Der Zug hatte Verspätung, deshalb bin ich zu spät gekommen.
examples.en: The train was delayed, that's why I arrived late.

examples.de: Sie spricht sehr gut Deutsch, deshalb hat sie den Job bekommen.
examples.en: She speaks German very well, that's why she got the job.

sentenceStructure.notes: 'Deshalb' occupies position 1, so the verb must come in position 2 (inversion). The subject moves after the verb. This is different from English where 'therefore' doesn't change word order.

usageTips: Remember: deshalb introduces the RESULT, not the reason
Can also be placed in the middle: 'Ich bleibe deshalb zu Hause'

commonMistakes.incorrect: Es regnet, deshalb ich bleibe zu Hause.
commonMistakes.correct: Es regnet, deshalb bleibe ich zu Hause.
commonMistakes.explanation: 'deshalb' takes Position 1 in its clause, pushing the verb to Position 2 and the subject to Position 3 (inversion).

values: Slightly more common in casual spoken German

title: deswegen
summary: Synonym for 'deshalb' — means 'therefore' or 'that's why'. Slightly more casual. Same grammar rules.

examples.de: Ich war müde, deswegen bin ich früh ins Bett gegangen.
examples.en: I was tired, that's why I went to bed early.

examples.de: Das Restaurant war voll, deswegen haben wir Pizza bestellt.
examples.en: The restaurant was full, that's why we ordered pizza.

examples.de: Er hat kein Auto, deswegen fährt er mit dem Bus.
examples.en: He doesn't have a car, that's why he takes the bus.

examples.de: Die Miete ist zu teuer, deswegen suchen wir eine neue Wohnung.
examples.en: The rent is too expensive, that's why we're looking for a new apartment.

sentenceStructure.notes: Grammatically identical to 'deshalb'. 'Deswegen' is slightly more common in everyday spoken German, while 'deshalb' is neutral across registers.

usageTips: Completely interchangeable with 'deshalb' — same meaning, same grammar
Slightly more common in casual spoken German
Same inversion rule: verb comes right after 'deswegen'
If in doubt, 'deshalb' and 'deswegen' are both always correct

commonMistakes.incorrect: Ich war krank, deswegen ich bin nicht gekommen.
commonMistakes.correct: Ich war krank, deswegen bin ich nicht gekommen.
commonMistakes.explanation: Like all connecting adverbs, 'deswegen' causes inversion. The verb must immediately follow it.

title: daher
summary: A consequence connector ('therefore' or 'hence'). Tends to be more common in formal or written German, though it can still appear in speech.

examples.de: Die Studie zeigt klare Ergebnisse, daher empfehlen wir eine Änderung.
examples.en: The study shows clear results, hence we recommend a change.

examples.de: Er hat viel Erfahrung, daher ist er der beste Kandidat.
examples.en: He has a lot of experience, therefore he is the best candidate.

examples.de: Die Straßen sind glatt, daher sollten Sie vorsichtig fahren.
examples.en: The roads are slippery, therefore you should drive carefully.

examples.de: Das Budget ist begrenzt, daher müssen wir Prioritäten setzen.
examples.en: The budget is limited, hence we need to set priorities.

sentenceStructure.notes: Same inversion rule as 'deshalb' and 'deswegen'. It tends to appear more frequently in academic writing, news articles, and professional communication, but this is a tendency, not a strict rule. Note: 'daher' can also mean 'from there' in a spatial sense.

usageTips: Often preferred in formal writing, academic papers, and professional emails
Same grammar as 'deshalb' and 'deswegen' — position 1, verb in position 2
Can also mean 'from there' (spatial) — 'Ich komme daher' = 'I come from there'
While formal, it's not strictly restricted to writing; you will still hear it in speech

commonMistakes.incorrect: Das Budget ist klein, daher wir müssen sparen.
commonMistakes.correct: Das Budget ist klein, daher müssen wir sparen.
commonMistakes.explanation: 'daher' requires the verb to come directly after it in Position 2.
